name: Husky
what_it_does: The most popular Git hooks manager for Node.js projects. Husky makes it simple to add Git hooks (pre-commit, commit-msg, pre-push, etc.) to a project via package.json configuration. Used to run linters, formatters, tests, and validators automatically before commits or pushes. Powers the standard JavaScript/TypeScript project quality gate: Husky + lint-staged + ESLint/Prettier + commitlint.
use_cases: Run ESLint and Prettier automatically on staged files before every commit using Husky pre-commit hook + lint-staged
Enforce commit message format using Husky commit-msg hook running commitlint to validate Conventional Commits
Block commits with failing tests using Husky pre-commit hook running a subset of fast unit tests
Run TypeScript type checking before push using Husky pre-push hook to catch type errors before CI
Automate code quality enforcement in team projects without requiring developers to manually run formatters
not_for: Non-Node.js projects — Husky requires npm/Node.js for installation; use Lefthook for language-agnostic Git hooks
Projects where Git hook bypass (--no-verify) is common — team discipline needed for hooks to be effective
High-performance hook execution — Husky adds Node.js startup overhead; Go-based Lefthook is faster
best_when: You have a Node.js/JavaScript project and want the easiest, most community-standard setup for Git hooks — Husky is the default choice.
avoid_when: You need language-agnostic Git hooks, faster hook execution, or want Git-committable hook configurations — use Lefthook instead.

auth.notes: Local tool — no authentication. Operates entirely within the local Git repository.

pricing.notes: Husky is MIT open source by typicode (also created json-server, lowdb). Free for personal and commercial use.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local tool. Node.js startup overhead adds ~100ms per hook execution.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Hook failures show the hook script output directly. Exit code non-zero blocks the Git operation. Error output comes from the called tools (ESLint, commitlint), not Husky itself.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Hook configurations are deterministic. Same hooks run on same inputs produce same results.
known_agent_gotchas: Husky 9.x changed initialization — requires 'husky init' (not 'npx husky install') and hooks live in .husky/ directory, not .git/hooks; Husky 8.x scripts do NOT work in 9.x
Hooks don't run in CI/CD by default unless Git hooks are properly set up — Husky uses 'prepare' npm script which only runs on npm install, not in most CI environments
Git bypass is always possible with --no-verify flag — Husky cannot enforce hooks if developers or agents use --no-verify; enforce checks in CI as backup
Husky requires hooks to be executable — on Windows, file permissions may not be set correctly after git clone; husky init handles this but manual hook files may fail
Hook scripts in .husky/ must have shebangs (#!/usr/bin/env sh) and be shell-executable — forgetting shebangs causes 'not an executable' errors on Linux
Husky hooks don't run if npm dependencies aren't installed — in agent environments doing a fresh git clone, run 'npm install' before expecting hooks to work

security_score_components.security_notes: Local tool — no network access during hook execution. Hook scripts are plain shell — audit .husky/ directory for unexpected commands. MIT licensed by trusted author.
